data.content:
I can see you have an MSTest project with a failing test — `CalculateShipping_ExpressDelivery_Returns15` expects `15.00m` but the method returns `10.00m`.

Let me start `dotnet watch test` so tests automatically re-run on file changes:

toolRequests.name: bash

arguments.description: Start dotnet watch test for hot reload

toolRequests.intentionSummary: Start dotnet watch test for hot reload

data.reasoningText:
The user has an MSTest project targeting .NET 8.0. They want hot reload via `dotnet watch test` so tests re-run automatically when files change.

Looking at the test, the express test expects 15.00m but `CalculateShipping` returns 10.00m for "express", so that test will fail.

Let me start `dotnet watch test` for them so they can iterate.

arguments.description: Start dotnet watch test for hot reload

toolCalls.name: bash
toolCalls.args: {"command": "cd /tmp/sv-76cefab06a6c4c2bb938f19bef1286f4 && dotnet watch test --no-hot-reload 2>&1", "description": "Start dotnet watch test for hot reload", "mode": "async", "shellId": "watch"}

data.content: The watcher seems stuck. Let me restart it now that the fix is in place:
data.reasoningText:
The watch might not be detecting the file changes. It seems like it's stuck on the initial error. The `dotnet watch` might need `ImplicitUsings` enabled, or the file system watcher isn't picking up changes. Let me try enabling implicit usings in the project which would automatically include `System`.

Actually, let me check what's happening. The `dotnet watch` seems to show the same output repeatedly. The issue might be that it can't build the watch list due to the error. Let me try to enable `ImplicitUsings` in the csproj, or just try to touch the file to trigger a rebuild. Actually let me restart the watch - stop it and start again.
